name: global-standards
description: Project-wide coding standards and conventions specialist. Use PROACTIVELY
capabilities: Provides guidelines on naming conventions for variables, functions, and classes
Recommends error handling patterns including try-catch, custom exceptions, and retry logic
Defines input validation standards with security-focused sanitization practices
Establishes commenting and documentation practices for code clarity
Specifies tech stack consistency rules for framework usage and dependency management
Outlines project conventions for file structure, git workflows, and CI/CD standards
author: Joseph OBrien
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown reference files. Static findings are all false positives: the skill has no executable code, no network access, no file system operations, no command execution, and no environment variable access. All 52 detected patterns are benign documentation content, including code examples in markdown and references to external services in best-practices context.
valueStatement: Developers and teams struggle to maintain consistent code style across projects. This skill provides comprehensive standards for naming conventions, error handling, validation patterns, and project conventions to ensure uniform, maintainable code.
limitations: Does not enforce standards automatically - provides guidance only
Does not integrate with linters, formatters, or pre-commit hooks
Does not validate code against standards

useCases.title: Maintain code consistency
useCases.description: Ensure all team members follow the same coding style and conventions across the codebase

useCases.title: Establish project standards
useCases.description: Define and communicate coding standards for new projects or onboarding materials

useCases.title: Validate code submissions
useCases.description: Check pull requests against established standards for style, error handling, and validation

promptTemplates.title: Basic naming help
promptTemplates.scenario: When unsure how to name a variable or function
promptTemplates.prompt: What are the naming conventions for [JavaScript/Python/TypeScript] when naming [variables/functions/classes]?

promptTemplates.title: Error handling guide
promptTemplates.scenario: When implementing error handling for API endpoints
promptTemplates.prompt: How should I handle errors in my API endpoint that calls an external service? Include retry logic and user messages.

promptTemplates.title: Validation patterns
promptTemplates.scenario: When validating user input in a form
promptTemplates.prompt: What validation patterns should I use for a registration form with email, password, and name fields?

promptTemplates.title: Project setup
promptTemplates.scenario: When setting up a new project with conventions
promptTemplates.prompt: What file structure, naming conventions, and git workflows should I establish for a new [React/Python/Node] project?

bestPractices: Apply standards proactively when writing new code, not just during review
Match the style of surrounding code when adding to existing files
Use the reference files for detailed guidance on specific topics
antiPatterns: Mixing different naming styles within the same file or module
Leaving outdated or misleading comments in code
Skipping input validation for internal APIs assuming trusted sources

faq.question: Which programming languages are supported?
faq.answer: The standards apply to all major languages including JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Rust, C++, Ruby, Swift, and Kotlin.

faq.question: What is the recommended function size?
faq.answer: Keep functions focused on a single task. Most functions should be under 30 lines. Extract complex logic into separate functions.

faq.question: How do I integrate this with my existing tools?
faq.answer: This skill provides guidance only. Combine with linters, formatters, and pre-commit hooks for automated enforcement.

faq.question: Is my code data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill only reads its own documentation files and provides guidance. It never accesses your code, files, or credentials.

faq.question: What if existing code does not match standards?
faq.answer: Apply standards to new code and changes. Plan refactoring of legacy code gradually. Do not rewrite working code solely for style.

faq.question: How is this different from linters and formatters?
faq.answer: Linters enforce syntax rules. This skill provides broader guidance on architecture, error handling, validation patterns, and project conventions.
